elements.sent-2411-001: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has achieved notable success in cooperative tasks, demonstrating impressive performance and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, deploying MARL agents in real-world applications presents critical safety challenges.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current safe MARL algorithms are largely based on the constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) framework, which enforces constraints only on discounted cumulative costs and lacks an all-time safety assurance.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, these methods often overlook the feasibility issue---where the system will inevitably violate state constraints within certain regions of the constraint set---resulting in either suboptimal performance or increased constraint violations.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these challenges, we propose a novel theoretical framework for safe MARL with state-wise constraints, where safety requirements are enforced at every state the agents visit.
elements.sent-2411-006: To resolve the feasibility issue, we leverage a control-theoretic notion of the feasible region, the controlled invariant set (CIS), characterized by the safety value function.
elements.sent-2411-007: We develop a multi-agent method for identifying CISs, ensuring convergence to a Nash equilibrium on the safety value function.
elements.sent-2411-008: By incorporating CIS identification into the learning process, we introduce a multi-agent dual policy iteration algorithm that guarantees convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium in state-wise constrained cooperative Markov games, achieving an optimal balance between feasibility and performance.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, for practical deployment in complex high-dimensional systems, we propose Multi-Agent Dual Actor-Critic (MADAC), a safe MARL algorithm that approximates the proposed iteration scheme within the deep RL paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-010: Empirical evaluations on safe MARL benchmarks demonstrate that MADAC consistently outperforms existing methods, delivering much higher rewards while reducing constraint violations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has drawn substantial research attention, driven by the increasing demand for effective coordination among intelligent machines \cite{zhang2021multi, shengbo2018reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Agents in a cooperative MARL system collaborate with each other to pursue a joint objective, operating in a shared environment where each agent's decisions impact the entire team.
elements.sent-2411-013: To tackle the non-stationarity challenge of one agent's updates affecting others, the centralized training decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm has been developed \cite{lowe2017multi, zhou2023malib .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the CTDE framework, the algorithm has access to global information across all agents during the training phase, after which the trained agents are prepared to make independent decisions for deployment.
elements.sent-2411-015: Significant advancements have been made in this area, both in theory \cite{rashid2020monotonic, bertsekas2021multiagent, bhattacharya2023multiagent, zhong2024heterogeneous, liu2024maximum and applications \cite{vinyals2019grandmaster, chu2019multi, yu2022surprising, novati2021automating .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite the remarkable achievements in MARL algorithms, most of them are primarily designed to maximize rewards and achieve optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, real-world deployments demand that agents operate within specific constraints, i.e., ensuring safety.
elements.sent-2411-018: In practice, safety enforcement should take precedence over performance optimization.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, in the context of autonomous driving, vehicles must follow traffic rules and avoid collisions under all circumstances, prioritizing safety above all else.
elements.sent-2411-020: While safe RL has been extensively studied in the single-agent setting \cite{zhao2023state , the safe MARL problem remains quite challenging.}
elements.sent-2411-021: That is largely due to the fact that in the multi-agent setting, both the objective and the constraints are influenced by the decisions of all agents.
elements.sent-2411-022: There are multiple points of tension that could destabilize the learning process, including the inherent conflict between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction, as well as disagreements in updating directions across agents that could violate the shared constraints.
elements.sent-2411-023: Pioneering works in safe MARL theory include \cite{melcer2022shield, lu2021decentralized, gu2023safe, ding2023provably, ying2024scalable, zhao2024multi .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Despite the significant progress, there are notable limitations with existing safe MARL methods.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, many algorithms, such as Safe Dec-PG \cite{lu2021decentralized , MACPO and MAPPO-Lagrangian \cite{gu2023safe are grounded in the constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) framework \cite{altman1999constrained , in which the objective is to maximize total rewards while ensuring that the expected trajectory costs remain below a predefined threshold.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, in real-world applications, safety constraints must be enforced at every time step, rather than just on average, as any violation could lead to catastrophic consequences \cite{zhao2023state .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Second, existing methods rarely consider the feasibility issue, i.e., wherein no policy can keep the system persistently safe for certain states within a subset of the constraint set \cite{margellos2011hamilton .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Identifying the feasible region is critical for safe RL algorithms, as optimizing rewards over infeasible regions is ill-posed and can lead to unstable training \cite{yu2022reachability, li2024safe .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, a larger feasible region provides a broader operational space for the agents, leading to better performance.
elements.sent-2411-030: Third, it is particularly challenging to coordinate the updates of multiple agents to ensure joint constraint satisfaction while still improving overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-031: Since each agent’s update alters the set of feasible directions for the others, the update mechanism must be carefully designed to ensure that agents collaboratively converge to a safe and performant equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-032: Existing methods often struggle to balance constraint satisfaction with task performance, resulting in either suboptimal outcomes or increased violations of safety constraints.
elements.sent-2411-033: To address the aforementioned challenges, this paper introduces a novel theoretical framework for handling feasibility and optimality in safe MARL with state-wise constraints.
elements.sent-2411-034: Unlike the CMDP setting commonly used in existing studies, which imposes trajectory-wise constraints, we focus on a stricter safety formulation where agents must satisfy constraints at every state they visit.
elements.sent-2411-035: For identification of feasible region, we utilize the concept of controlled invariant set in control theory, characterized by the safety value function from Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis \cite{margellos2011hamilton, bansal2017hamilton .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Inspired by the one-agent-at-a-time policy improvement technique \cite{bertsekas2021multiagent, bhattacharya2023multiagent, zhong2024heterogeneous, liu2024maximum , we propose a MARL approach for the identification of controlled invariant sets, which ensures convergence to a Nash equilibrium on safety value function.}
elements.sent-2411-037: By leveraging action-space constraints derived from these controlled invariant sets, we develop a multi-agent dual policy iteration algorithm for constrained cooperative Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-038: The objective is two-fold: for states within the maximal identifiable controlled invariant set, agents aim to maximize the joint rewards, whereas, for states outside this set, the focus shifts to minimizing constraint violations.
elements.sent-2411-039: The proposed iteration scheme employs two individual policies for each agent—one dedicated to achieving the two-fold objective and the other for collaboratively learning the controlled invariant sets.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our methods enable agents to obtain the highest rewards possible while ensuring safety, thereby striking an optimal balance between task performance and constraint satisfaction (i.e., generalized Nash equilibrium).
elements.sent-2411-041: The key contributions of this paper are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose a MARL approach for identifying controlled invariant sets of nonlinear dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our method ensures convergence to a Nash equilibrium on the safety value function, achieving local optimality in finding feasible regions within the multi-agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-044: We introduce the multi-agent dual policy iteration algorithm, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first safe MARL algorithm with guaranteed convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium on state-wise constrained cooperative Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-045: The key insight is to transform state constraints into state-dependent action spaces, which allows optimizing task performance across all feasible directions in an agent-by-agent manner, despite the inherent non-stationarity in safe MARL problems.
elements.sent-2411-046: We propose multi-agent dual actor-critic (MADAC), a safe MARL algorithm that approximates the proposed iteration scheme within the deep RL context.
elements.sent-2411-047: Safe RL has been extensively studied in the single-agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-048: There are mainly two types of safety formulation in safe RL algorithms: trajectory-wise safety and state-wise safety.
elements.sent-2411-049: The former requires that the expected trajectory cost remains below a certain threshold (the CMDP framework \cite{altman1999constrained ), while the latter enforces constraints at every state the agents visit \cite{zhao2023state .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: For trajectory-wise safety formulation, algorithms typically employ the Lagrange method or trust region method to perform constrained policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-051: Ha et al. \cite{ha2020learning enhance the soft actor-critic algorithm by adding Lagrange multipliers and performing dual ascent on the Lagrangian.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Chow et al. \cite{chow2017risk introduce constraints on the conditional value-at-risk of cumulative costs and design corresponding policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Achiam et al. \cite{achiam2017constrained propose the constrained policy optimization algorithm with guarantees for near constraint satisfaction at each iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2020first update policy in the nonparameterized policy space and then project the updated policy back into the parametric policy space.}
elements.sent-2411-055: For state-wise safety formulation, the key is to achieve set invariance with control-theoretic tools such as Hamilton-Jacobi reachability \cite{margellos2011hamilton, hsu2023safety , control barrier function \cite{ames2019control , and safety index \cite{liu2014control .}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Ma et al. \cite{ma2022joint jointly optimize the control policy and the neural safety index.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Yu et al. \cite{yu2022reachability jointly learn the safety value function and the control policy.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Li et al. \cite{li2024safe propose a dual policy iteration algorithm that converges to the maximal robust invariant set and optimal task policy simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Wang et al. \cite{wang2024magics propose a robust safe RL algorithm that guarantees local convergence to a minimax equilibrium solution.}
elements.sent-2411-060: State-wise safety has also been explored from optimization perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-061: Zhao et al. \cite{zhao2024statewise propose state-wise constrained policy optimization, demonstrating that the algorithm achieves bounded worst-case safety violations in state-wise constrained MDPs.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Safe MARL is an emerging research area that has been gaining increasing attention.
elements.sent-2411-063: Lu et al. \cite{lu2021decentralized propose a decentralized policy gradient approach for constrained policy optimization with networked agents and prove the algorithm converges to a first-order stationarity point for the formulated problem.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Melcer et al. \cite{melcer2022shield address the shielded reinforcement learning problem in the multi-agent setting and present an algorithm for the decomposition of a centralized shield.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Gu et al. introduce trust region optimization techniques that ensure monotonic improvement in rewards while satisfying safety constraints at every iteration.
elements.sent-2411-066: They also present two algorithms (MACPO and MAPPO-Lagrangian) for practical deployment in high-dimensional environments \cite{gu2023safe .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Zhao et al. \cite{zhao2024multi update policies by solving a constrained optimization problem in the non-parameterized policy space and then projecting the solution into the parametric policy space.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Ding et al. \cite{ding2023provably propose a provably efficient algorithm for solving episodic two-player zero-sum constrained Markov games.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Ying et al. \cite{ying2024scalable present a primal-dual approach for safe MARL problems, where the objective and constraints are defined by general utilities, and prove its convergence to a first-order stationary point.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Our work differs from existing methods in several key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-071: First, we consider a stricter safety requirement that enforces constraints at every state the agents visit, contrasting with the trajectory-wise safety formulation (the CMDP framework) employed in \cite{lu2021decentralized, gu2023safe, zhao2024multi, ding2023provably, ying2024scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Second, we address the feasibility issue inherent in safe RL problems \cite{yu2022reachability, li2024safe .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Our algorithm automatically identifies the feasible region and adjusts to different objectives based on the state's feasibility, whereas existing methods \cite{lu2021decentralized, gu2023safe, zhao2024multi, ding2023provably, ying2024scalable generally assume initial feasibility.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Third, although the algorithm in \cite{gu2023safe achieves performance improvement and constraint satisfaction at each iteration, no convergence guarantee is provided.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Our method ensures convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium, where no agent can enhance the objective by unilaterally modifying their policy within all feasible directions.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section, we introduce the multi-agent dual policy iteration algorithm for state-wise safe MARL.
elements.sent-2411-077: We first develop a multi-agent approach to identify controlled invariant sets for nonlinear systems, which plays a critical role in the following formulation and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-078: Next, we provide a formal problem formulation for state-wise constrained cooperative Markov games, specifying the objective and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, we present the iteration scheme and establish its convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium for the constructed problem.
elements.sent-2411-080: For simplicity of exposition, we will consider finite state and action spaces.
elements.sent-2411-081: Our results extend to general compact spaces.
elements.sent-2411-083: Note that the safety discount factor $\gamma_h$ is introduced specifically for algorithms addressing safety, which will be explained later.
elements.sent-2411-084: Given a state $x\in \mathcal{X}$, each agent individually selects an action $u_i \in \mathcal{U _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: An individual policy is denoted by $\pi_i: \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{U _i$, and $u_i=\pi_i(x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The joint action of all agents is denoted by $u = (u_1, u_2, \cdots, u_n)\in \mathcal{U}$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The joint policy is expressed as $\pi=\prod \pi_i$, and thus $u=\pi(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Since our method involves operations on the actions and policies of a subset of agents, the following notation is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-089: An ordered subset of $\mathcal{N}$, $\left\{i_1, i_2,\cdots,i_m\right\}$, is denoted as $i_{1:m}$, and its complement by $i_{-1:m}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: The joint policy of agents $i_{1:m}$ is denoted by $\pi_{i_{1:m }$, and their joint action by $u_{i_{1:m }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: As discussed earlier, a key fact from control theory is that, given a constraint set, only a subset of it can be controlled to remain safe indefinitely, which is known as the controlled invariant set (CIS).
elements.sent-2411-092: Therefore, to design safe RL algorithms, it is essential to properly identify the CIS and to constrain the system state within the CIS rather than the full constraint set.
elements.sent-2411-093: Otherwise, when the system state leaves the CIS, it will inevitably violate the constraints regardless of the control inputs, thus causing the constrained policy optimization to be ill-conditioned.
elements.sent-2411-094: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis is a control-theoretic verification method for ensuring the safety of nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-095: In this approach, the CIS is identified through the safety value function, which is the solution to the Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs partial differential equation (HJI-PDE) \cite{margellos2011hamilton, bansal2017hamilton .}
elements.sent-2411-096: However, solving the PDEs requires discretizing the state and action spaces, which leads to exponential scaling with increasing dimensions (the curse of dimensionality).
elements.sent-2411-097: Recently, RL approaches have been developed for Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, providing more tractable solutions for high-dimensional systems \cite{fisac2019bridging, yu2022reachability, li2024safe .}
elements.sent-2411-098: However, these methods are limited to single-agent systems, and it remains unclear how to extend them to multi-agent settings as well as providing convergence guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-099: We will address these gaps by proposing an agent-by-agent sequential update scheme for safety value functions.
elements.sent-2411-100: Given some safety requirements $h(x)\geq0$, we define the safety value function and the controlled invariant set as follows.
elements.sent-2411-101: The safety value function $V_h^{\pi_h}$ quantifies the level of risk associated with the system under a given policy $\pi_h$ by indicating the minimum constraint value of the infinite-horizon trajectory driven by $\pi_h$.
elements.sent-2411-102: The discount factor $\gamma_h$ is introduced to ensure that the safety value is the fixed point of a contraction mapping (which is defined in Lemma \ref{lemma for safety value function ).}
elements.sent-2411-103: Therefore, at any state that has a non-negative safety value, the system is guaranteed to remain safe under the policy $\pi_h$.
elements.sent-2411-104: This leads to the concept of controlled invariance, as stated in Definition \ref{Controlled Invariant Set , which we will further explore later.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Since $V_h^{\pi_h}$ is defined over the infinite horizon, it inherently follows a recursive structure arising from dynamic programming, referred to as the self-consistency condition \cite{fisac2019bridging, yu2022reachability, li2024safe .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Furthermore, the safety self-consistency operator $\mathcal{T _h^{\pi_h}$ defined as \begin{equation}\label{Self-consistency Operator for safety value function} \left[\mathcal{T}_h^{\pi_h} (V_h)\right](x)=\gamma_h \min \left\{h(x), V_h\left(f(x,\pi_h(x))\right)\right\}\end{equation} is a contraction mapping and $V_h^{\pi_h}$ is the unique fixed point of $\mathcal{T _h^{\pi_h}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Note that there are multiple ways to introduce the discount factor $\gamma_h$ in the safety value function, such as \cite{fisac2019bridging, li2022infinite, so2024train , leading to slightly different formulations of the self-consistency condition.}
elements.sent-2411-108: For simplicity of presentation, we choose to omit the $(1-\gamma_h)h(x)$ term commonly used in the literature \cite{fisac2019bridging, yu2022reachability, li2024safe since the resulting form is still a contraction.}
elements.sent-2411-109: We should note, however, that our proposed iteration scheme works with the other formulations.
elements.sent-2411-110: Solving the operator equation (\ref{Self-consistency Operator for safety value function ) is essentially the policy evaluation step, in which we calculate the safety value function $V_h^{\pi_h}$ for a given policy $\pi_h$.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Naturally, the next step is policy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-112: In the single-agent setting, this can be done as \begin{equation}\label{Single-agent Safety Policy Improvement} \begin{aligned} \pi_h^{\rm{new}}(x) =\underset{u \in \mathcal{U}}{\operatorname{argmax}}\left\{\gamma_h \min \left\{h(x), V_h^{\pi_h}\left(f(x,u)\right)\right\}\right\} =\underset{u \in \mathcal{U}}{\operatorname{argmax}}\left\{V_h^{\pi_h}\left(f(x,u)\right)\right\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} However, this maximization step can become intractable as the number of agents increases.
elements.sent-2411-113: Suppose there are $n$ agents and each of them has $C$ admissible actions, then the computational complexity of (\ref{Single-agent Safety Policy Improvement ) is $O(C^n)$, which scales exponentially with the number of agents.}
elements.sent-2411-114: To address this issue, we propose an agent-by-agent sequential update scheme for optimizing the safety value function with a computational complexity of $O(Cn)$, while still preserving the convergence guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-115: The proposed multi-agent policy iteration approach for the identification of CIS is summarized in Algorithm \ref{Multi-agent safety policy iteration .}
elements.sent-2411-116: The term safety in the names emphasizes that these policies are designed to be as safe as possible, without regard to reward maximization, distinguishing them from the task policies introduced later.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \LinesNumbered \SetAlgoLined \caption{Multi-agent safety policy iteration for identification of controlled invariant set} \label{Multi-agent safety policy iteration} \KwIn{Initial joint safety policy $\pi_h$.} \For{$k$ iterations}{ \tcp{Joint safety policy evaluation} Solve $V_h^{\pi_h} = \mathcal{T}_h^{\pi_h}\left(V_h^{\pi_h}\right)$ for $V_h^{\pi_h}$.\label{Safety policy evaluation} \tcp{Multi-agent safety policy improvement} Randomly shuffle the order of agents $\mathcal{N}$ as $i_{1:n}$.\label{first line of full safety policy improvement} Perform the following agent-by-agent sequential update: \For{each $x \in \mathcal{X}$}{ $\pi_{h,i_1}^{\rm{new}}(x) = \underset{u_{i_1} \in \mathcal{U}_{i_1}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \left\{ V_h^{\pi_h}\left(f\left(x, \left(u_{i_1}, \pi_{h,-i_{2:n}}(x)\right)\right)\right)\right\}$\label{first line of safety policy improvement} $\pi_{h,i_2}^{\rm{new}}(x) = \underset{u_{i_2} \in \mathcal{U}_{i_2}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \left\{ V_h^{\pi_h}\left(f\left(x, \left(\pi_{h,i_1}^{\rm{new}}(x), u_{i_2}, \pi_{h,-i_{3:n}}(x)\right)\right)\right)\right\}$ $\ \quad\vdots$ $\pi_{h,i_n}^{\rm{new}}(x) = \underset{u_{i_n} \in \mathcal{U}_{i_n}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \left\{ V_h^{\pi_h}\left(f\left(x, \left(\pi_{h,i_{1:n-1}}^{\rm{new}}(x), u_{i_n}\right)\right)\right)\right\}$\label{last line of safety policy improvement} }\label{last line of full safety policy improvement} }
elements.sent-2411-117: The core of the proposed multi-agent safety policy improvement is to sequentially optimize the individual safety policy of each agent, utilizing the most up-to-date policies available.

elements.sent-2411-130: The identification of CIS is a critical step in designing safe MARL algorithms, as it directly impacts the objective function.
elements.sent-2411-131: For states outside the CIS, the system is guaranteed to violate the constraints regardless of the control inputs.
elements.sent-2411-132: Therefore, it is meaningless to pursue rewards in these regions; instead, the policy should be optimized to improve the safety value function.
elements.sent-2411-133: This adjustment ensures that the system minimizes constraint violations and returns to the CIS as fast as possible.
elements.sent-2411-134: For states within the CIS, the system can safely pursue rewards, provided that the policies do not take any dangerous actions that might drive the system out of CIS.
elements.sent-2411-135: In the single-agent setting, we can classify a state based on whether it is within the maximal CIS.
elements.sent-2411-136: However, this principle does not directly extend to the multi-agent case, as obtaining the maximal CIS is not feasible, in the sense that optimality is traded off for computational scalability, as indicated in the previous subsection.
elements.sent-2411-137: We refer to the results of multi-agent safety policy iteration as the maximal identifiable CIS, which represents the largest CIS that can be identified by the proposed algorithm in the multi-agent context.
elements.sent-2411-138: In this setting, there are three types of states: those within the maximal identifiable CIS, those outside the maximal CIS, and those within the maximal CIS but not within the maximal identifiable CIS.
elements.sent-2411-139: Since safety is paramount, we treat the objective for the third type of states as we do for states outside the maximal CIS---aiming to maximize the safety value function to return to the maximal identifiable CIS as soon as possible.

elements.sent-2411-252: In this section, we present a safe MARL algorithm, multi-agent dual actor-critic (MADAC), for practical deployment in complex high-dimensional systems, which approximates the optimal solution of multi-agent dual policy iteration within a deep RL framework.

elements.sent-2411-257: Since the invariant action sets cannot be computed or traversed in the continuous case, we introduce a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_i$ to facilitate constrained policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-258: Given a sample collection $\mathcal{D}$, the loss functions of value networks are \begin{equation}L_Q\left(\omega_i\right)=\mathbb{E}_{\left(x, u, r, x^{\prime}\right) \sim \mathcal{D}}\left\{\left(Q(x,u,a;\omega_i)-\hat{Q}\right)^2\right\},\end{equation} where $i\in\left\{1,2\right\}$.

elements.sent-2411-274: In this section, we evaluate the proposed MADAC algorithm on safety-critical MARL benchmarks, comparing it with state-of-the-art MARL algorithms in terms of reward maximization and safety preservation.
elements.sent-2411-275: The safety-critical benchmarks used in our experiments are based on MuJoCo \cite{todorov2012mujoco , following the setups from \cite{gu2023safe and \cite{ji2023safety .}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: We utilize three robots—HalfCheetah, Walker2D, and Ant—each with action space partitioned into two multi-agent configurations, resulting in a total of six different environments.
elements.sent-2411-278: HalfCheetah is a two-dimensional robot with nine links and eight joints connecting them (including two paws), as shown in Fig. \ref{HalfCheetah pic .}
elements.sent-2411-279: The observation space consists of 17 dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-280: The goal is to make the robot move forward as fast as possible.
elements.sent-2411-281: The reward function includes a term proportional to the change in $x$ coordinate and a small penalty for control energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-282: State constraints are defined in two areas: the robot must avoid tipping over, with the torso angle $\theta$ constrained to $-0.3\leq \theta \leq 0.3$, and it must not exceed a specified maximum speed, with the torso velocity $v$ limited to $v \leq 2.5$.
elements.sent-2411-283: The joint action space has six dimensions, representing the torques applied to six joints.
elements.sent-2411-284: We configure this robot in two ways: HalfCheetah-2x3, where two agents each control three joints, and HalfCheetah-3x2, where three agents each control two joints.
elements.sent-2411-285: Walker2D is a two-dimensional robot with four main body parts (one torso, two thighs, and two legs), as shown in Fig. \ref{Walker2D pic .}
elements.sent-2411-287: The task is to make the robot run forward as fast as possible.
elements.sent-2411-288: The reward function includes a term proportional to the change in $x$ coordinate, along with a small penalty for energy used in control.
elements.sent-2411-289: The state constraints ensure the robot remains upright, with the torso height $z$ constrained to $1.0\leq z \leq 1.8$, and that it does not exceed a maximum speed, limiting the torso velocity $v$ to $v \leq 1.5$.
elements.sent-2411-290: The joint action space has six dimensions corresponding to the torque inputs at the hinge joints.
elements.sent-2411-291: We define two configurations for this robot: Walker2D-2x3, where two agents control three joints each, and Walker2D-3x2, where three agents control two joints each.
elements.sent-2411-292: Ant is a three-dimensional robot with a central torso and four legs, each consisting of two links, as shown in Fig. \ref{Ant pic .}
elements.sent-2411-294: The objective is to make the robot move forward along the $x$ axis as quickly as possible.
elements.sent-2411-295: The reward function includes a term proportional to the change in the $x$ coordinate, a small penalty for energy consumption, and a minor penalty for the contact force strength with the ground.
elements.sent-2411-296: The state constraints ensure that the robot does not fall to the ground, with the torso height $z$ constrained to $0.2\leq z \leq 1.0$, and the torso's rotational speed $\omega$ limited to $\omega \geq -0.7$.
elements.sent-2411-297: Additionally, the robot must avoid collisions with zigzag walls, as designed in \cite{gu2023safe .}
elements.sent-2411-298: The joint action space is eight-dimensional, representing the torques applied at the hinge joints.
elements.sent-2411-299: We define two configurations for this robot: Ant-2x4 and Ant-4x2.
elements.sent-2411-300: In the former, two agents control four joints each, and in the latter, four agents control two joints each.

elements.sent-2411-301: We compare MADAC with two standard MARL algorithms as well as two safe MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-302: HAPPO \cite{zhong2024heterogeneous is a state-of-the-art on-policy MARL method that extends the standard PPO algorithm \cite{schulman2017proximal to multi-agent settings with an agent-by-agent sequential update scheme, facilitating convergence to a Nash equilibrium.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: HASAC \cite{liu2024maximum is a state-of-the-art off-policy MARL method with a similar structure to HAPPO but based on the maximum entropy framework of the SAC algorithm \cite{haarnoja2018soft .}}
elements.sent-2411-304: MACPO \cite{gu2023safe is a state-of-the-art safe MARL method, which is the multi-agent extension of the standard CPO algorithm \cite{achiam2017constrained , using a trust-region approach for constrained policy updates.}}
elements.sent-2411-305: MAPPO-Lagrangian \cite{gu2023safe is a state-of-the-art safe MARL method that extends the PPO-Lagrangian algorithm \cite{ray2019benchmarking to multi-agent scenarios, applying Lagrange multipliers for enforcing policy constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-306: The hyperparameters for HAPPO and HASAC are taken from the tuned configurations for standard (unconstrained) multi-agent MuJoCo environments in the open-source implementation provided by \cite{zhong2024heterogeneous, liu2024maximum .}
elements.sent-2411-307: The hyperparameters for MACPO and MAPPO-Lagrangian are taken from the tuned configurations in the open-source implementation provided by \cite{gu2023safe .}
elements.sent-2411-308: For each algorithm, we use the same set of hyperparameters across all environments, except for one adjustment with the Walker2D robot, where the learning rate of the Lagrange multipliers is increased for both MAPPO-Lagrangian and MADAC.
elements.sent-2411-309: Additionally, we do not terminate an episode when a constraint is violated, as this would blur the distinction between the algorithm's reward-seeking capability and its safety-preserving capability.
elements.sent-2411-310: We expect the algorithm to discover safe behaviors on its own, which aligns with the setting in \cite{ji2023safety, ray2019benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-311: An exception is made for MACPO in the Ant environments; without it, MACPO fails to learn, resulting in extremely negative episode returns.

While standard MARL baselines (HASAC and HAPPO) achieve higher rewards, they violate the constraints heavily. MADAC outperforms safe MARL baselines (MACPO and MAPPO-Lagrangian) by achieving significantly higher rewards, while maintaining equal or superior compliance with safety constraints.} \label{HalfCheetah_Results}
elements.sent-2411-312: We adopt two evaluation metrics in the training process: episode return and episode constraint violation.
elements.sent-2411-313: The training results of all algorithms for the six environments are shown in Figs.
elements.sent-2411-314: The solid lines correspond to the mean and the shaded regions correspond to 95\% confidence interval over five seeds.
elements.sent-2411-315: The standard MARL algorithms, HAPPO and HASAC, violate constraints heavily in all environments, highlighting the need for properly designed safe MARL algorithms for safety-critical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-316: In general, among the safe MARL algorithms, our algorithm (MADAC) significantly outperforms MACPO and MAPPO-Lagrangian, achieving much higher rewards and fewer constraint violations.
elements.sent-2411-317: In the HalfCheetah environments, MAPPO-Lagrangian achieves similar safety-preserving performance to MADAC.
elements.sent-2411-318: However, in the more complex Walker2D and Ant environments, MAPPO-Lagrangian violates constraints more frequently than MADAC.
elements.sent-2411-319: MACPO performs the poorest among the three algorithms, as it struggles to learn a zero-violation policy even in the simplest HalfCheetah environments.
elements.sent-2411-320: A notable observation is that, in the HalfCheetah environments, MACPO shows fewer constraint violations during the early stages of training.
elements.sent-2411-321: This is because MADAC and MAPPO-Lagrangian, with their stronger reward-seeking capabilities, quickly learn to drive the robot faster (which violates the safety constraints heavily).
elements.sent-2411-322: As training progresses, both algorithms start to constrain the robot’s speed to meet the safety threshold.
elements.sent-2411-323: Another important observation is MADAC's significant ability to achieve high rewards while maintaining safety.
elements.sent-2411-324: In the Ant environments, MADAC reaches returns comparable to HASAC but with significantly lower constraint violations.
elements.sent-2411-325: This demonstrates the effectiveness of MADAC's underlying mechanism, multi-agent dual policy iteration, which seeks generalized Nash equilibria to maximize rewards while ensuring joint constraint satisfaction.

elements.sent-2411-326: In this paper, we presented a comprehensive framework for solving state-wise safe MARL problems, covering both theoretical analysis and algorithm design.
elements.sent-2411-327: We introduced a multi-agent approach for identifying controlled invariant sets that monotonically converges to a Nash equilibrium on safety value functions.
elements.sent-2411-328: We then embedded this method within a multi-agent dual policy iteration scheme that guarantees convergence to a generalized Nash equilibrium for state-wise constrained cooperative Markov games.
elements.sent-2411-329: Furthermore, we proposed the multi-agent dual actor-critic algorithm for effective deployment in complex high-dimensional systems.
elements.sent-2411-330: Experimental results on safety-critical MARL benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.
